preamble: In English and other Germanic languages, the voiceless velar stop /k/ is aspirated [kʰ] in syllable-initial position and unaspirated [k] after /s/ or in unstressed syllables. This is classic allophony: the two variants are in complementary distribution and never contrast within a single language variety. The aspiration is predictable from context and not perceived by native speakers as a difference. Note: in Hindi, Mandarin, and other languages, /k/ and /kʰ/ are distinct phonemes — not allophones — and that phonemic contrast should not be confused with the allophonic pattern described here.

examples.note: 'kit' [kʰɪt] vs. 'skit' [skɪt]: /k/ is aspirated word-initially but unaspirated after /s/. Both are realizations of the same phoneme /k/.
Standard German aspirates /k/ syllable-initially: 'kalt' [kʰalt] 'cold'. Unaspirated in consonant clusters.
Included for contrast: in Mandarin, [k] and [kʰ] are separate phonemes — the opposite of the English allophonic pattern.

sources: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics. Harcourt.
Roach, P. (2009). English Phonetics and Phonology. Cambridge University Press.
context: Syllable-initial before stressed vowel = aspirated [kʰ]; post-/s/ = unaspirated [k]
